summary: This notebook provides a baseline training pipeline for the BirdCLEF 2025 competition, demonstrating how to convert raw audio recordings into mel spectrograms and train a multi-label classification model using PyTorch. It highlights a flexible data loading strategy that supports both pre-computed spectrograms for speed and on-the-fly generation, alongside standard audio-to-image augmentations and mixup regularization. The author emphasizes reproducibility through fixed seeds and a structured configuration class, while offering a debug mode for rapid experimentation. Readers should take away a practical, end-to-end recipe for audio classification baselines, including proper handling of variable-length audio, multi-label encoding, and stratified cross-validation.
competition_flow: Loads raw audio and metadata, converts them to 256x256 mel spectrograms, trains an EfficientNet-B0 model with multi-label BCE loss across 5 stratified folds, and saves per-fold checkpoints.
data_reading: Reads train.csv and taxonomy.csv via pandas.
Optionally loads pre-computed mel spectrograms from a .npy file when LOAD_DATA=True.
Uses librosa.load for on-the-fly audio parsing when spectrograms are not pre-computed.
data_processing: Converts audio to mel spectrograms (128 bins, 1024 FFT, 512 hop, 50-14kHz range).
Normalizes spectrograms to [0,1] and resizes to 256x256.
Pads or truncates audio to a fixed 5-second center crop.
Applies time/frequency masking and brightness/contrast augmentations with 50% probability.
insights: Pre-computed mel spectrograms significantly reduce training time compared to on-the-fly generation.
Mixup regularization and spectrogram augmentations help mitigate overfitting on multi-label audio data.
Stratified cross-validation is essential for preserving class distribution in imbalanced bird sound datasets.
Handling variable-length audio via center-cropping and padding ensures consistent input dimensions for CNNs.
